reasoning: The ICE BofA CCC & Lower US High Yield Index saw a rapid surge in yields from 12.42% on Feb 20, 2026 to 13.0% by Feb 28, mirroring a significant late-February stock market selloff. News searches confirmed this selloff was prompted by hot PPI inflation data and tariff concerns (the Dow dropped 800+ points on Feb 23, with more losses on Feb 26-27). Sharp, aggressive spikes in credit yields often represent localized panic repricing. Absent an evolving systemic credit crisis, they frequently mean-revert or stabilize rather than march linearly higher over the ensuing 1-4 weeks. The FRED statistical tool projected a ~37% probability of the yield exceeding 13.0 by Mar 8. Given the clear macroeconomic catalysts (hot inflation/rates + tariff jitters), I have adjusted this base rate slightly higher to 42% to account for short-term momentum. For the March 31 horizon, I follow the guideline to increase uncertainty for distant dates, drawing the probability closer to 50% (47%), acknowledging the wide range of potential outcomes over a 1-month window.
